topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The click of the mouse was the loudest sound in the quiet apartment. Liam stared at the screen, his breath catching somewhere in his chest. It was the essay he’d poured his soul into—the one about navigating a childhood illness and finding unexpected resilience. Instead of his humble submission, the screen displayed a polished, eloquent version of his narrative, complete with a glowing university logo. His hands began to shake, a tremor that made the keyboard feel alien beneath his fingertips. He leaned back in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and squeezed his eyes shut, trying to force the image away. When he opened them, the words swam before him, mocking his efforts. A cold sweat prickled his hairline. He scrambled to minimize the window, his heart hammering a frantic rhythm against his ribs, every muscle in his body locked in a rigid tension.

[story 2]

Elara felt the blood drain from her face as she read the document. It was her personal story, the one she’d painstakingly crafted over sleepless nights, now sitting there, perfectly formatted, labeled ‘Example Submission.’ A sharp, metallic taste flooded her mouth. She dropped the laptop onto the rug with a muffled thud, the sound echoing unnervingly in the small room. Her chest constricted, making it difficult to draw a full breath. She rose slowly, her legs feeling unsteady, and began pacing the perimeter of the living room, her gaze darting from the screen to the closed door, then back again. Each footfall seemed amplified, a clumsy, desperate attempt to escape the suffocating space the image had created.

[story 3]

Marcus froze mid-sip of his lukewarm coffee. The email attachment he’d downloaded—his finalized college essay—was not his. It was a masterpiece, dripping with sophisticated vocabulary and an emotional depth that felt utterly foreign. His knuckles turned white where he gripped the mug, the ceramic slick with nervous perspiration. He slowly lowered the cup, his eyes fixed on the text. A wave of nausea washed over him, heavy and sickening. He started to breathe in shallow, rapid gasps, his throat tightening until the air felt thin. He backed away from the desk, bumping into the bookshelf, the sudden movement jarring him into a state of profound stillness, as if waiting for an invisible blow to land.
